title: Add prediction mode — 'What happens next?' before every step across all concepts
description: THE single most impactful practice feature. Before each step, pause and show a multiple-choice question: CPU Scheduling: 'Which process runs next?' (2-4 options). Page Replacement: 'Will this be a HIT or FAULT?' + 'Which page will be evicted?' Deadlock: 'Can this process finish?' Memory: 'TLB hit, PT hit, or page fault?' Thread Sync: 'Will this thread acquire or block?' Student selects, then sees if they're right with explanation. Research: testing effect improves retention by 50-80% (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006).
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle for 'Predict Mode' in step controls for all concepts with step-through
Before each step, prediction prompt appears with 2-4 options
CPU Scheduling: 'Which process runs next? A) P1 B) P2 C) P3 D) CPU Idle'
Page Replacement: 'HIT or FAULT?' then if FAULT: 'Which page evicted?'
Memory: 'TLB Hit / PT Hit / Page Fault?' — user selects before seeing result
Immediate feedback: correct = green check + brief WHY. Wrong = red X + explanation using visualization state
Score tracker: 'You predicted 7/10 correctly (70%)' per simulation run
Prediction does NOT slow down non-predict mode — toggle is off by default

title: Add 'Be the Algorithm' manual tracing mode for CPU scheduling
description: Transform passive watching into active doing. In tracing mode, the visualization shows the current state (ready queue, running process, time) and the student must CLICK which process runs next. FCFS: click the process with earliest arrival. SJF: click the one with shortest burst. Priority: click the highest priority. Wrong click = red flash + explanation. Right = green + advance.
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle for 'Trace Mode' in CPU scheduling controls
Ready queue shown as clickable process cards
Student clicks the process they think the algorithm would select
Correct: green animation, step advances, 'Right! FCFS picks P1 because it arrived at t=0'
Wrong: red flash, explanation: 'FCFS picks by arrival time, not burst. P1 arrived first.'
Score: 'You traced 8/12 steps correctly'
Works for FCFS, SJF, RR (which process AND when preempted), Priority

title: Add 'Be the Algorithm' manual tracing for page replacement
description: Show current frame contents and next page reference. Student must: (1) Decide HIT or FAULT, (2) If FAULT, click which frame to evict. For LRU: student must track recency. For FIFO: student must track insertion order. For Optimal: student must look ahead in reference string (shown). Wrong eviction choice = explanation of the algorithm's actual rule.
acceptanceCriteria: Frame cells are clickable in trace mode
Student first clicks HIT or FAULT button
If FAULT: student clicks the frame to evict
Correct eviction: 'Right! FIFO evicts frame 0 because page 7 was loaded first (oldest).'
Wrong eviction: 'FIFO evicts the OLDEST loaded page, not the least recently used. Frame 0 has page 7, loaded at t=0.'
Reference string visible with future entries (for Optimal)
Score tracker per algorithm

title: Add comparison challenges — 'Which algorithm wins on this data?'
description: Before running Compare All, present a challenge: 'This process set has one very long job (burst=50) and three short ones (burst=2). Which scheduling algorithm will have the LOWEST average wait time? A) FCFS B) SJF C) Round Robin D) Priority.' Student answers, then Compare All runs to verify. 5 challenges per concept category.
acceptanceCriteria: Challenge card appears when student clicks a 'Challenge' button
Scenario described in plain English with the data visible
Multiple choice: 2-4 algorithm options
After answering, Compare All runs automatically to show the actual result
Feedback: 'You chose SJF — correct! SJF minimizes average wait by running short jobs first. The Convoy Effect in FCFS would have caused 48+ tick waits.'
5 challenges for scheduling, 3 for page replacement, 3 for allocation

title: Add scenario-based selection challenges — 'Which algorithm for this real-world problem?'
description: 10 real-world scenarios where the student must choose the right OS concept/algorithm. Examples: 'Web server handling 10K concurrent requests — which scheduling?' 'Database with 20GB working set, 4GB RAM — which page replacement?' 'Two microservices deadlocking on database locks — which prevention strategy?' Each scenario has 3-4 options with explanations.
acceptanceCriteria: 10 scenario cards accessible from module header or sidebar
Each scenario describes a real-world situation in 2-3 sentences
3-4 options with only one best answer
Feedback explains WHY the answer is best AND why the wrong answers are wrong
Scenarios span all 6 concept categories
Difficulty labeled (Easy/Medium/Hard)

title: Add debugging challenges — 'Find the bug in this implementation'
description: Show broken pseudocode alongside the visualization showing wrong behavior. Student must identify which line is wrong. 3 challenges per concept area. Examples: (1) FCFS that doesn't handle idle time. (2) LRU that doesn't update access time on HIT. (3) Mutex that allows any thread to release (no ownership). The visualization RUNS with the bug, showing the incorrect result — student must find the root cause.
acceptanceCriteria: 6-9 debugging challenges across all concept areas
Broken pseudocode displayed alongside running visualization
Visualization runs with the bug (showing wrong output)
Student clicks the buggy line or selects from multiple choice
Feedback: 'Line 5 should update access time on HIT. Without it, recently accessed pages look old and get evicted incorrectly.'
Fixed version shown after correct identification

title: Add worst-case input crafting challenge — 'Break the algorithm'
description: Inversion of normal learning: instead of 'watch algorithm work', challenge student to craft the WORST input. 'Make FCFS have the highest average wait time possible with 4 processes.' 'Find a reference string where FIFO produces MORE faults with 4 frames than 3.' 'Create a resource allocation that deadlocks with the fewest processes.' Student enters input, runs algorithm, system scores how 'broken' the result is.
acceptanceCriteria: Challenge mode toggle for each concept
Objective stated clearly: 'Maximize average wait time' or 'Maximize page faults'
Student enters custom input
Algorithm runs, score computed based on objective metric
Leaderboard: 'Your worst case: avg wait 15.3. Best worst case found: 22.1'
Explanation after: 'The worst case for FCFS is when the longest job arrives first (Convoy Effect)'

title: Add quick-fire recall quiz for OS concepts (Coffman conditions, complexity, terminology)
description: Flashcard-style rapid quiz: 'Name the 4 deadlock conditions', 'Time complexity of LRU page replacement?', 'What does TLB stand for?', 'Is FIFO a stack algorithm?'. 20 questions per quiz, timed, scored. Covers terminology, complexity, algorithm properties across all 6 concepts. Based on the testing effect: retrieval practice improves long-term retention by 50-80%.
acceptanceCriteria: Quiz mode accessible from module header
20 questions randomly selected from pool of 50+
Mix of: multiple choice, true/false, fill-in-blank
Timer: 30 seconds per question
Score: correct count + time bonus
Review mode: show all wrong answers with correct answers + explanations
Question pool covers all 6 concept areas proportionally

title: Add feedback system with hints for wrong answers in all practice modes
description: Wrong answers should teach, not just reject. 3-level hint system: Level 1: Rephrase the question. Level 2: Give a clue about the algorithm's strategy. Level 3: Show the first step of reasoning. All wrong-answer feedback uses the visualization state: 'You predicted P2 would run next, but look at the ready queue — P1 arrived at t=0 and FCFS picks by arrival order.'
acceptanceCriteria: Every wrong answer shows WHY it's wrong, not just 'Incorrect'
Hint button available with 3 progressive levels
Feedback references the current visualization state
Correct answers also explain WHY (reinforce, don't just validate)
Point penalty for using hints (fewer points per hint level used)

title: Add scoring and progress tracking — per-concept mastery, streaks, accuracy
description: Track: per-concept mastery level (0-5 stars based on prediction accuracy), overall module completion %, streak counter (consecutive correct predictions), accuracy per algorithm, weakness identification ('You struggle with LRU eviction — practice more'). Store in localStorage. Visible in sidebar and module header.
acceptanceCriteria: Per-concept mastery: 0-5 stars shown next to each concept in sidebar
Streak counter: consecutive correct predictions (resets on wrong answer)
Accuracy breakdown per algorithm in properties panel
Weakness identification: 'You got 3/10 on LRU predictions — review LRU eviction logic'
All stats persist via localStorage
Reset button to clear progress

title: Add progressive difficulty system — warm-up → easy → medium → hard → expert
description: 5-level difficulty ladder for practice. Warm-up: recall facts ('What is FCFS?'). Easy: predict next step. Medium: compare algorithms on data. Hard: find bugs in pseudocode. Expert: craft worst-case inputs. Student starts at warm-up, unlocks next level at 80% accuracy. Each level maps to a Bloom's taxonomy level.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 difficulty levels clearly labeled in practice UI
Level 1 (Warm-up): recall questions — terminology, complexity, properties
Level 2 (Easy): predict next step in single algorithm
Level 3 (Medium): comparison challenges — which algorithm wins?
Level 4 (Hard): debugging challenges + scenario selection
Level 5 (Expert): worst-case crafting + algorithm design
Unlock at 80% accuracy on current level
Visual progress bar showing level completion

title: Add OS Daily Challenge — one new scenario-based question per day
description: Adapt the existing daily challenge system (interview/daily-challenge.ts) for OS concepts. One question per day, seeded by date so all users get the same one. Difficulty varies by day of week (Mon=easy, Fri=hard). Questions span all 6 concepts. Streak tracking: consecutive days answered. Reuse the DailyChallenge interface and hashString deterministic selection.
acceptanceCriteria: One OS question per day, same for all users (deterministic date hash)
Difficulty varies: Mon=Level 1, Tue=Level 2, Wed=Level 3, Thu=Level 4, Fri=Level 5
Question types: prediction, comparison, scenario, debugging (rotating)
Streak counter: consecutive days with a correct answer
Notification in module header: 'Daily Challenge available!'
Reuses daily-challenge.ts infrastructure

title: Add address translation tracing exercise — 'Be the MMU'
description: Manual tracing for virtual memory: given a virtual address, student must: (1) Split into page number + offset, (2) Check TLB (shown), decide TLB hit/miss, (3) If miss, check page table (shown), decide PT hit/fault, (4) Compute physical address. Each step is a separate input. System verifies each step independently with feedback.
acceptanceCriteria: Exercise mode in Memory Management concept
Step 1: Enter page number and offset for given VA and page size
Step 2: Check TLB display, answer 'HIT or MISS?'
Step 3: If miss, check page table display, answer 'Valid or Page Fault?'
Step 4: If valid, compute physical address (frame * page_size + offset)
Feedback at each step: 'Correct! VA 34 / page_size 16 = page 2, remainder 2.'
Multiple exercises with different parameters

title: Add Coffman conditions identification challenge for deadlock scenarios
description: Present 5 scenarios (some deadlocked, some not). For each, student must: (1) Identify if deadlock exists (yes/no), (2) If yes, name which Coffman conditions are met, (3) Suggest which condition to break to prevent it. Feedback shows the RAG visualization proving or disproving the student's answer.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 pre-built scenarios with varying deadlock states
Student selects: Deadlock / No Deadlock
If deadlock: checkbox for each Coffman condition met
Prevention suggestion: dropdown for which condition to break
Feedback: runs detectDeadlock() and shows RAG with cycle highlighted
Explanation: 'Correct — circular wait exists: P0→R1→P1→R2→P0'
